title: Reflection
objectives: Understand reflection through a real-life analogy
Inspect and manipulate classes/methods/fields at runtime
See how frameworks (Spring, Hibernate, JUnit) use reflection
Recognize the performance and security trade-offs

sections.markdown:
**Imagine a well-organized library.**

- Normally, you go to a shelf and take a specific book by title. You know exactly what you're looking for. That's how your compiled code works — you call `user.getName()` because you knew about `getName()` at compile time.
- But sometimes a librarian walks around with a **card catalog** — inspecting shelves, listing every book, checking each book's dust jacket for what it's about, and then borrowing a book they'd never seen before. They can do this because the library exposes its structure via the catalog.

**Reflection is the card catalog for Java classes.** At runtime, you can ask any class 'what methods do you have?', 'what fields?', 'what annotations?' — and then call, read, or write them, even ones the compiler never saw. That's how Spring wires `@Autowired`, how JUnit finds `@Test` methods, how Jackson turns a POJO into JSON without any hard-coded knowledge of the class.

**Reflection** is the ability of a program to examine and modify its own structure at runtime — classes, methods, fields, annotations. It's how frameworks discover, load, and connect your code without knowing about it at compile time.
## The core Reflection API
## Why frameworks LOVE reflection

rows: Scan @Component classes, inject dependencies (@Autowired), wire beans, apply AOP
Map @Entity classes to tables; populate fields from ResultSets
Serialize/deserialize any POJO by inspecting its fields
Generate HTTP clients from annotated interfaces
Similar patterns via annotation processing (compile-time, not runtime)

sections.markdown: **The insight worth quoting:** frameworks feel magical because they use reflection to discover *your* classes and act on them. Once you understand this, `@Autowired` and `@Test` stop being mysteries — Spring is literally scanning your classpath, checking for annotations, and invoking methods by name. Understanding this demystifies every 'how does the framework know...' question.
## A complete sample program — build a tiny DI container in 40 lines
## Common beginner mistakes

**Mistake 1: Using reflection in ordinary application code.**

Reflection loses compile-time type checking (typos in method names crash at runtime instead of compile time), is significantly slower than direct calls, and can break under the Java Platform Module System. Save it for frameworks and truly dynamic scenarios. In your business code, prefer interfaces and direct calls.

**Mistake 2: Not caching `Method` / `Field` lookups.**

`cls.getMethod("name", ...)` is not cheap — it iterates every method every call. If you'll invoke the same method thousands of times, look it up **once** and store it. Real DI containers all do this.

**Mistake 3: `setAccessible(true)` everywhere.**

Bypassing encapsulation is a code smell in app code — it means you're touching private state that the class's authors did not intend to expose. In frameworks it's necessary; in your business logic it usually indicates a bad API. Also, JPMS (Java 9+) restricts `setAccessible` — modules must open their packages.
## Best practices
**1. Use reflection to build frameworks — not app code.** If a colleague can achieve the goal without reflection, avoid it.
**2. Cache Method / Field lookups.** They're expensive to look up but cheap to reuse.
**3. Prefer annotations for discovery, not string identifiers.** `@Test` is safer than a naming convention like `testFoo()`.
**4. Consider `MethodHandle` for hot paths.** It can be as fast as direct calls after warm-up (JIT can inline it).
**5. Consider annotation processing (compile-time reflection).** Libraries like Lombok, MapStruct, Dagger generate code at compile time — same expressiveness, no runtime cost.
## Practice programs

**Practice 1 (Easy) — List everything about a class.** Given `MyClass.class`, print all fields (name + type), all methods (name + return type + params), all annotations.

**Why:** You'll internalize the API.

**Practice 2 (Medium) — Call by name.** Write a method `Object invoke(Object obj, String methodName, Object... args)` that finds and invokes the right method.

**Why:** This is the shape of every scripting-to-Java bridge.

**Practice 3 (Hard) — Tiny DI container.** Build the sample program in this topic. Extend it to support constructor injection instead of only field injection.

**Why:** You'll understand Spring's magic once and for all.

**Practice 4 (Expert) — Mini JSON serializer.** Given any POJO, use reflection to write it to JSON — inspect each field, format based on type, recurse for nested objects.

**Why:** This is how Jackson/Gson work under the hood.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is reflection, and when is it used?**

**A:** Reflection is Java's ability to inspect and manipulate its own program structure at runtime — discover classes, methods, fields, and annotations without knowing about them at compile time. **Primary use:** frameworks. Spring uses reflection to scan for `@Component` classes and wire `@Autowired` dependencies. JUnit uses it to discover and run `@Test` methods. Jackson uses it to serialize any POJO. Hibernate uses it to map entities to database rows. Application code should almost never use reflection directly — it loses compile-time safety and is slower. But understanding it demystifies how every major Java framework works underneath.

**Q2: How do Spring's `@Autowired` and JUnit's `@Test` work under the hood?**

**A:** Both use reflection to discover and act on your annotated code. **Spring startup:** scans classpath for classes annotated with `@Component` / `@Service` / `@Repository`. For each, it uses reflection to instantiate the class, then iterates fields looking for `@Autowired`. For each `@Autowired` field, it looks up an appropriate bean (or constructs one) and sets the field via `Field.setAccessible(true); field.set(instance, bean)`. **JUnit test run:** scans your test class for methods annotated `@Test`. For each, it creates a fresh instance of the test class, then invokes the method via `method.invoke(testInstance)`. Both frameworks are basically 'walk the class, find annotations, do the right thing.' Understanding this makes framework behavior no longer magical.

**Q3: What are the downsides of reflection?**

**A:** Four main ones. **(1) No compile-time type safety** — a typo in a method name crashes at runtime, not compile time. **(2) Performance overhead** — a reflective method call is much slower than a direct call (though the JIT can inline reflective calls after warm-up, closing much of the gap). **(3) Breaks encapsulation** — `setAccessible(true)` accesses private state and can cause bugs if the target class changes internals. **(4) Module system restrictions** (Java 9+) — modules must explicitly `opens` their packages for reflection to work; deep reflection into `java.base` is often forbidden. So: don't use reflection in application business logic. Save it for frameworks and dynamic scenarios where the flexibility genuinely outweighs the costs.

**Q4: What does `setAccessible(true)` do, and what are the risks?**

**A:** By default, reflection respects Java's access modifiers — you can't call a private method or read a private field. `setAccessible(true)` on a `Field` / `Method` / `Constructor` bypasses these checks, letting you touch private members. **Uses:** frameworks (Spring, Jackson, Hibernate) need it to work with your private fields. **Risks:** (1) breaks encapsulation, so if the target class changes its internals, your code breaks; (2) can violate `SecurityManager` policies (deprecated in Java 17 but still relevant in some environments); (3) in Java 9+, modules must explicitly `opens` their packages, or `setAccessible` throws `InaccessibleObjectException`. In app code, `setAccessible` is a smell — it usually means the API you're calling doesn't support your use case; a better fix is often to change the API.

**Q5: What's the difference between `Class.forName("...")` and `ClassName.class`?**

**A:** **`ClassName.class`** is a compile-time literal — the class must exist and be visible at compile time. Fastest and safest. **`Class.forName("...")`** takes a fully-qualified class name as a string and loads the class at runtime — no compile-time dependency. Useful when you're loading plugins, deserializing polymorphic JSON, or otherwise dynamically discovering classes. `Class.forName` also triggers static initialization by default (there's a 3-arg overload that skips it). Trade-off: `.class` is safer and faster; `forName` is more dynamic. Rule: use `.class` in normal code; use `forName` only when the class name is genuinely unknown at compile time.

**Q6: What's the difference between reflection and `MethodHandles`?**

**A:** Both let you invoke methods by looking them up at runtime. **Classic reflection** (`Method.invoke`) is the older API — flexible but slower; each invocation goes through several layers of security checks and boxes primitive arguments. **`MethodHandles`** (`java.lang.invoke`, Java 7+) is a newer API designed for the JVM's `invokedynamic` instruction. Method handles can be as fast as direct calls after JIT warm-up — the JIT can inline them. Use `MethodHandles` when performance matters (like a language runtime or a hot reflective path). Use classic reflection for one-off discovery. In practice, most application-level frameworks still use `Method` + `Field` because performance is 'good enough' and the API is simpler.

**Q7: How do annotations interact with reflection?**

**A:** Annotations by themselves do nothing — they're just metadata. To be useful at runtime, they must have `@Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME)`; only then can reflection see them via `element.getAnnotation(Ann.class)` or `isAnnotationPresent(Ann.class)`. A framework then walks classes/methods/fields, checks for its annotations, and acts on them. This is how `@Autowired` works (Spring), `@Test` (JUnit), `@JsonProperty` (Jackson), and thousands of others. Alternatively, annotations with `@Retention(SOURCE)` are used by *annotation processors* at compile time (Lombok, MapStruct, Dagger) — same expressiveness with no runtime cost, but requires build-time codegen.

## Real-world examples

- **Spring Framework** — the entire IoC container is built on reflection.
- **JUnit 5** — discovering test methods, running lifecycle callbacks, injecting parameters.
- **Jackson / Gson** — turning any POJO into JSON without pre-configuration.
- **JPA / Hibernate** — mapping objects to database rows.
- **Micronaut and Quarkus** invert this — they generate code at build time to avoid runtime reflection, giving them faster startup and lower memory.
- **Kotlin coroutines** use reflection sparingly but rely on it for continuation handling in some cases.

## Advanced notes

- **`invokedynamic`** — the JVM bytecode instruction used for lambdas, string concatenation, and dynamic languages. `MethodHandle` is its native currency.
- **Annotation processing** — compile-time reflection alternative. `javax.annotation.processing.AbstractProcessor`. Lombok, MapStruct, Dagger, Room all do this.
- **`Proxy` and `InvocationHandler`** — create classes on the fly implementing a set of interfaces. How Spring AOP and mocking libraries work.
- **ClassGraph / Reflections** — libraries that make classpath scanning easier.
- **JPMS + reflection** — Java 9+ modules restrict deep reflection. Use `opens` in `module-info.java` to allow it.
- **Ahead-of-time (AOT) compilation** (GraalVM Native Image) — struggles with reflection because everything is decided at build time. Frameworks like Micronaut solve this by generating code instead.
- **JEP 471** — some old `sun.misc.Unsafe` and reflection APIs are gradually being deprecated / removed in favor of safer alternatives.

flashcards.back: Runtime inspection and manipulation of classes, methods, fields, and annotations. Basis of every Java framework.
Spring (@Autowired), JUnit (@Test), Jackson/Gson (JSON), Hibernate (@Entity), Retrofit — nearly all of them.
Slower, no compile-time type safety, breaks encapsulation, restricted by JPMS in Java 9+.
Bypasses private access checks. Necessary for frameworks; a smell in app code.
MethodHandles (java.lang.invoke) can be as fast as direct calls after JIT; classic reflection is slower but more flexible.

quiz.explain: Frameworks (Spring, JUnit, Jackson) — not application code.

options: Reflection scanning fields for the annotation
quiz.explain: Spring inspects fields at runtime and injects matching beans.

options: forName loads dynamically at runtime by string; .class is compile-time

quiz.q: For fast repeated reflective calls, prefer…
options: Cached MethodHandle or Method after JIT warm-up
quiz.explain: Cache lookups; MethodHandle can be near-direct-call speed.

quiz.q: In Java 9+, deep reflection into another module requires…
options: The other module to `opens` its package
quiz.explain: JPMS enforces encapsulation across module boundaries.

challenges.text: Print all declared fields and methods of a given Class object (name + type + params).
Instantiate a class by name (`Class.forName(...).getDeclaredConstructor().newInstance()`) and call `toString()`.
Write `Object invoke(Object obj, String methodName, Object... args)` that dynamically finds and calls the right method.
Create a runtime annotation `@Persistent` and print every field of a POJO that has it.
Build a tiny DI container: scan a class for `@Inject` fields and populate them from a bean registry (see sample program).
Write a mini annotation-driven CLI: methods annotated `@Command("name")` become subcommands invoked by name.
Build a mini JSON serializer using reflection: given any POJO, walk its fields and emit valid JSON.

How do Spring's `@Autowired` and JUnit's `@Test` work under the hood?
What are the downsides of reflection?
What does `setAccessible(true)` do, and what are the risks?
What's the difference between reflection and `MethodHandles`?
How do annotations interact with reflection?
